children: In a 1-life PvP environment, the plan you make in the staging area decides the fight before a single shot is fired. You do not get a second attempt. This guide uses the Swedish squad leader methodology — a simple, repeatable loop for turning a vague objective into a clear, time-bound plan your squad can actually execute under pressure.
The whole process answers three questions, in order:
What is the problem? How much time do I have? How do we adapt when it breaks?

attrs.class: mt-10 mb-3 border-b border-white/10 pb-2 text-xl font-bold tracking-tight text-white

children: Before you talk about movement, get brutally clear on what you are actually being asked to do and what stands in the way. If you cannot state the objective in one sentence, you are not ready to brief it.
What does winning look like — hold a grid, destroy an asset, break the enemy's main effort? Name it.
How many, how equipped, armor or air? Where are they likely strong, and where are they thin?
What covers our approach, what channels us into a kill zone, and what high ground matters?
Squad size, weapon teams, vehicles, and — critically — how much daylight and time you have.

attrs.class: my-6 rounded-2xl border border-l-4 p-4 shadow-lg backdrop-blur-md bg-error/10 border-error
mb-1 font-mono text-xs font-bold tracking-widest uppercase text-error-alert

children: they do. Build your plan around their most dangerous option, then exploit the gaps.

attrs.class: mt-10 mb-3 border-b border-white/10 pb-2 text-xl font-bold tracking-tight text-white

children: This is the part most squad leads skip, and it is the part that wins matches. Work
from the moment you expect contact and assign hard times to every step. A plan without a clock is just a wish.
Set your decisive moment — call it

attrs.class: rounded bg-black/40 px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono text-[0.85em] text-primary

children: (the assault, the ambush trigger, the objective seizure).

attrs.class: rounded bg-black/40 px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono text-[0.85em] text-primary
rounded bg-black/40 px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono text-[0.85em] text-primary
rounded bg-black/40 px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono text-[0.85em] text-primary

children: Reserve time for the things that always run long: crossing danger areas, re-org after contact, and getting everyone on the same map.
so every watch matches. "We move in 5" means nothing if nobody agrees on now.

attrs.class: my-6 rounded-2xl border border-l-4 p-4 shadow-lg backdrop-blur-md bg-primary/10 border-primary
mb-1 font-mono text-xs font-bold tracking-widest uppercase text-primary

children: Budget roughly a third of your available time for planning and rehearsal, and two-thirds for movement and execution. If you spend the whole window talking, you will be rushing — and loud — when it counts.

attrs.class: mt-10 mb-3 border-b border-white/10 pb-2 text-xl font-bold tracking-tight text-white

children: No plan survives first contact. The point of Phases 1 and 2 is not a rigid script — it is to give your squad enough shared understanding that they keep fighting
when the plan falls apart and you can't talk to them.
, not just instructions: "I want us holding the north ridge by

attrs.class: rounded bg-black/40 px-1.5 py-0.5 font-mono text-[0.85em] text-primary

children: , even if Alpha gets pinned." Tell them the
Push decisions down. A fireteam that understands the goal will make a good call faster than they can raise you on a (possibly looted) radio.
Name your triggers and branches in advance: "If we take fire from the treeline, Bravo suppresses, Alpha flanks left — no further orders needed."
Run a 60-second rehearsal or backbrief. Have a team lead repeat the plan back; you will catch the gaps before the enemy does.

attrs.class: my-6 rounded-2xl border border-l-4 p-4 shadow-lg backdrop-blur-md bg-tactical-yellow/10 border-tactical-yellow
mb-1 font-mono text-xs font-bold tracking-widest uppercase text-tactical-yellow

children: When the plan breaks, the worst choice is to freeze and wait for perfect information. Make a decision, communicate it in one line, and keep the squad moving. Momentum beats hesitation in a 1-life fight.
